# Architectural Claims and Design Loops {#sec-architecture-20-ontology}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"All models are wrong, but some are useful."*
>
> — George E. P. Box, *Robustness in the Strategy of Scientific Model Building* (1979)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** George E. P. Box, a British statistician who made major contributions to time-series analysis, famously noted that while all models are flawed, the good ones are practically useful. For architecture work, fast proxies and surrogate models are useful only when their scope, calibration, and failure modes are explicit; they can accelerate bounded exploration, but they do not by themselves justify commitment.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This chapter introduces how the book states and reviews architectural claims, along with its ontology and compact framework. Architecture 2.0 is the engineering discipline of making architectural claims and their design loops explicit enough that AI systems can act inside them credibly.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What does a design loop need to make explicit before an AI output can be
accepted or rejected as an architectural claim?
:::

Computer architecture has always depended on disciplined abstraction. An
architect rarely reasons directly from every transistor to every application
behavior. The field instead builds models, simulators, workload
characterizations, cost estimates, design rules, and review practices that make
large design spaces tractable. That quantitative tradition is central to
modern architecture practice [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach].
It also explains why Architecture 2.0 should not be framed as a sudden break
from the past. The continuity is the *existence* of the loop; the field has always designed through loops of abstraction,
measurement, feedback, and judgment. The discontinuity is its *automation*.
Historically, humans drove this loop; in Architecture 2.0, AI systems can
execute steps programmatically, including routine rejection and advancement
within authority declared by an accountable engineering organization. That
automation changes the failure mode. When verification and rejection mechanisms
collapse, plausible errors can propagate faster and reach higher commitment
levels before the accountable process stops them.

What becomes first-class alongside the artifact is the loop that produces and
tests claims about it. In Architecture 1.0, the architect uses tools to design
artifacts, such as an ISA extension, a cache hierarchy, an accelerator, a memory
system, a chiplet partition, a compiler policy, or a system configuration. But
an artifact matters because it supports an architectural claim. In Architecture 2.0, the architect must also design the
loop that produces, tests, rejects, and revises that claim.
The loop must expose what state it saw, what actions it allowed, what
alternatives it rejected, what evidence supports the result, and who owns the
commitment if the claim is wrong [@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20]. The
five-part execution state introduced in @sec-moonshot is a compact reading of
those obligations, not a form the reader must fill out. Without those pieces, an AI
system may still produce plausible text, code, or configurations, but it is not
participating in architecture work in a way the field should trust.

Other fields do not supply a ready-made Architecture 2.0 standard. They do show
which properties become explicit when claims, workflows, and consequences grow
too complex to carry in individual memory. Popper's falsifiability criterion
supplies one sharp starting point. A claim should expose what observation could
count against it, not only what evidence would make it look stronger.[^fn-popper-falsifiability]

[^fn-popper-falsifiability]: Popper developed the criterion in *Logik der
Forschung*, published in German in 1934 and later in English as *The Logic of
Scientific Discovery* [@Popper2002LogicScientificDiscovery]. Here
falsifiability belongs to an architectural claim and its test protocol, not to
an AI model as an intrinsic property. It asks what observable result would
conflict with the claim. It does not imply that one failed run establishes the
root cause or ends the inquiry.

| **External practice** | **What it makes explicit** | **What transfers to Architecture 2.0** | **Where the analogy stops** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Experimental science | A claim, test conditions, observations that could conflict with it, and provisional rather than final acceptance [@Popper2002LogicScientificDiscovery]. | State the claim and non-claim, predeclare counterevidence, and preserve results that weaken the preferred explanation. | Tool failure, model mismatch, and scope error can also explain a failed run, so rejection still requires diagnosis. |
| Systems safety and assurance | System constraints, process assumptions, control and feedback paths, evidence arguments, responsibility, and waivers [@Leveson2012EngineeringSaferWorld; @Kelly2004GSN]. | Review the whole sociotechnical loop rather than treating a capable model or passing component check as sufficient. | Most architecture exploration is not safety certification; evidence and independence should scale with consequence. |
| High-energy physics | Data, code, computational environment, workflow steps, provenance, versions, and internal review state captured during the analysis [@ChenEtAl2016CERNAnalysisPreservation]. | Preserve versioned design trajectories and runnable inputs while the work is happening, not as a reconstruction after publication. | A preserved workflow can support replay without establishing that its claim, baseline, or decision was adequate. |
| Clinical genomics | Independent reanalysis before a predictor's output is allowed to guide consequential enrollment or treatment [@BaggerlyCoombes2009]. | Seat credible challenge before expensive commitment and make data transformations inspectable. | Architecture decisions have different harms and regulatory structures, so the review institution cannot be copied wholesale. |
| Site-reliability engineering | Observability, error budgets, runbooks, rollback, and revalidation under drift [@BeyerEtAl2016SRE]. | Expand automated authority where actions are monitored and reversible, and narrow it as rollback becomes costly. | Silicon, interfaces, and long-lived hardware commitments are not as cheaply reversible as software deployments. |
| Scientific databases and benchmarks | Shared data, metadata, interfaces, workloads, and reporting rules that let results travel across groups [@JainEtAl2013MaterialsProject; @SPEC2017CPU; @MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. | Build common datasets and comparison protocols around explicit task and evidence scope. | A dataset or leaderboard alone does not record why a design was rejected or who authorized the resulting claim. |

: **Other fields converge on inspectable claims, workflows, evidence, challenge, and decisions.** Architecture 2.0 borrows these properties without importing another field's institutions or vocabulary unchanged. {#tbl-cross-field-method-lineage tbl-colwidths="[18,27,29,18]"}

@tbl-cross-field-method-lineage is a lineage map, not another checklist. It
also answers why a governed loop is more than retained logs. Logs can preserve
observations and provenance, but they do not by themselves state the
architectural claim, expose missing alternatives, establish an independent
challenge path, or authorize a decision. Those relationships are the method.
Architecture 2.0 makes them reviewable together rather than treating a pile of
traces as reasoning.

This chapter gives the reusable language for that shift. The goal is not to
classify every paper or tool. A taxonomy of current systems will age quickly.
The more durable contribution is a consistent way to state the architectural
claim being made, together with an ontology of the entities and relationships
that must exist before AI systems can act inside the architecture design loop
credibly.

The ontology has to earn its space by being useful. A researcher should be
able to cite it when explaining the structure of an Architecture 2.0
contribution. A reviewer should be able to use it to ask what state, action,
feedback, evidence, and rejection authority a paper exposes. A tool builder
should be able to use it as a checklist for a harness or environment. An author
should be able to use it to state the claim and loop for a concrete design
problem. If the ontology cannot support those uses, it is only vocabulary.
Its test is whether it exposes enough state for a generative method to act
within bounds and enough evidence for an accountable reviewer or declared
decision process to reject or advance the result.

The deeper reason we need such an ontology is that architecture reasoning still lacks a durable
claim-and-evidence layer above RTL. Below RTL, design flows already have artifacts that
tools can parse, transform, reject, and sign off. Although High-Level Synthesis (HLS) flows, modeling
languages, compiler IRs, and EDA frameworks structure parts of the path to
implementation, much of the
reasoning that decides *what* should be built still lives in whiteboards,
spreadsheets, scripts, slides, review memories, and natural-language specs. The
design-loop card gives a reviewer one index into that work. It can point to the
loop's tool contract (@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces) and
detailed evidence record without copying either into another reader-facing form. These
records are candidate structured abstractions for the part of architecture
practice that has not yet been made inspectable.

Compiler infrastructure gives a useful but limited analogy. MLIR, a multi-level intermediate representation framework for building compilers, made many compiler transformations inspectable
[@LattnerEtAl2020MLIR]. Architecture 2.0 is not asking for one universal
architecture IR. It is asking for a reviewable claim-and-evidence layer where
design intent, workload scope, feedback, and rejection can be checked before an
artifact is trusted.

The hierarchy in this chapter is simple. The artifact is what may eventually be
built. The architectural claim is what a reviewer accepts or rejects about that
artifact. The design loop is the process that tests the claim. The ontology names
the relationships the loop must expose. The design-loop card is the sole
practical reader-facing schema. Before a run it records the loop's promise;
afterward it records whether the loop kept that promise.

That layer sits between tacit reasoning and implementation flows
(@fig-structured-layer-above-rtl). Its purpose is not to add ceremony. It is to make the
part of architecture reasoning above RTL explicit enough that tools, automated participants,
reviewers, and architects can all see the same loop state.

![**Architecture 2.0 needs a structured layer above RTL:** Whiteboards, spreadsheets, and natural-language specs still carry much of the reasoning that decides what should be built. The intended loop, represented state, environment contract, and supporting evidence record turn that reasoning into a reviewable layer that can connect to simulators, compilers, RTL, EDA, and software evidence.](images/F4d-structured-layer-above-rtl){#fig-structured-layer-above-rtl width="100%" fig-alt="Three-layer diagram showing tacit architecture reasoning flowing into a represented design-loop layer, which then connects to executable implementation flows such as simulators, compilers, RTL, EDA, and signoff."}

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can turn an AI prompt, paper, tool, or project into a
reviewable design-loop card. That means you can:

- **State** a bounded architectural claim and identify the evidence needed to review it;
- **Trace** the loop that produced the claim through its actions, feedback, rejection, and commitment;
- **Use** the design-loop card as the single practical schema for reviewing an AI-assisted paper, tool, or project;
- **Recognize** the chapter's tuple, equation, figures, and grouped questions as explanatory views of that card rather than additional forms.
:::

## The Architectural Claim Is the Unit of Review

Asking whether a generative model[^fn-ml-generative-model] can design hardware is too coarse a framing. The more
architecture-native approach focuses on identifying the specific claim being made and determining the evidence required to make that claim credible. Architects rarely accept an artifact by itself. They
accept or reject a claim about that artifact relative to a workload, baseline,
design space, objectives, constraints, and evidence. The definition below names
that review object so the rest of the chapter can ask what state a loop must
carry before an AI-assisted result can be accepted or rejected.

[^fn-ml-generative-model]: A class of AI models designed to generate new data, such as text or configurations, that resembles its training data. In an architecture design loop such a model proposes candidate artifacts inside the legal design space, which is why the chapter insists its output still be tested as a claim rather than trusted as a result.

> **Architectural claim.** An architectural claim is a statement that a
> proposed artifact, method, or loop improves, preserves, or explains a
> hardware/software behavior for a specified workload or scenario relative to a
> baseline, under explicit objectives, constraints, evidence, rejection
> conditions, and decision authority.

> **Rejection authority and commitment boundary.** Rejection authority is the
> person, independent check, constraint, or rule empowered to block or weaken a
> claim. The commitment boundary is the strongest next decision that the current
> evidence permits. A method may execute either decision only within authority
> that the accountable organization has declared in advance.

The clause "for a specified workload or scenario" is doing real work, and a neighboring field paid to learn it.

::: {.callout-field-note title="Valid for the last rocket, not this one"}
The Ariane 5 rocket reused an inertial-reference software component that had flown correctly on Ariane 4. Its correctness claim was real, but it was a claim about Ariane 4's flight envelope. On Ariane 5's different, higher-velocity trajectory a value overflowed a conversion the older profile never reached, both channels shut down, and the vehicle self-destructed about forty seconds after launch [@Lions1996Ariane5]. The component was not broken. Its evidence simply did not cover the scenario it was now committed to.

**Lesson:** A claim carries the scenario it was verified under, so an AI-assisted loop that reuses a cheap, trusted component still has to re-check its evidence against the new workload, because the claim, not the code, is what has to hold.
:::

A plain-language version might say that this candidate improves useful work for
this workload, relative to this baseline, inside this design space, under these
constraints, using this evidence, and with this rejection authority. The
following tuple is optional notation for that sentence:
$$
\mathcal{C} =
\langle W, B, \mathcal{D}, \mathbf{J}, \mathcal{K}, E, R, M, H \rangle .
$$
Here, $W$ is the workload or scenario, $B$ is the baseline, $\mathcal{D}$
is the legal design space, $\mathbf{J}$ is the objective vector, $\mathcal{K}$
is the constraint set, $E$ is the evidence, $R$ is the rejection authority,
$M$ is the commitment boundary, and $H$ is the human or organizational decision
authority. The notation is a claim-focused view of the design-loop card, not a
second schema or a separate checklist. Its purpose is to prevent a generated
artifact from masquerading as an architectural result before the comparison,
constraints, evidence, rejection authority, and commitment boundary are visible.

@tbl-architectural-claim-schema applies that same claim-focused view to the
lighthouse prompt. It is an explanation of what the card must carry, not an
additional form for the reader.

| **Claim field** | **Reader question** | **Lighthouse instance** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Workload or scenario** | What behavior is the design supposed to serve? | XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workloads, with latency, sensing, graphics, and interaction requirements. |
| **Baseline** | Compared to what architecture, software stack, or prior result? | A scalar CPU-only baseline, a vector-capable CPU, an accelerator baseline, or an existing mobile XR subsystem. |
| **Design space** | What choices are legal, and which regions are invalid? | Open-standard RISC-V ISA options, vector width, CPU/accelerator partitioning, memory hierarchy, clocking, compiler/runtime path, and tool-flow limits. |
| **Objective vector** | What counts as improvement, and what tradeoffs matter? | Throughput, tail latency, energy, area, programmability, verification burden, and evidence cost under the 3\ W target. |
| **Constraints** | What cannot be violated even if a metric improves? | ISA compatibility, correctness, thermal limits, process assumptions, package limits, software compatibility, and 3\ nm-class low-power envelope. |
| **Evidence** | What supports the claim at the required commitment level? | Workload traces, simulations, power model, sensitivity checks, rejected candidates, tool logs, and comparison against baselines. |
| **Rejection authority** | What observation, tool, review, or rule can invalidate or weaken the result? | Missed latency target, power envelope violation, invalid RTL/configuration, compiler failure, simulator mismatch, or weak coverage. |
| **Commitment boundary** | What claim is the evidence strong enough to support, and what remains uncommitted? | Exploration, RTL study, implementation, deployment, or silicon-facing commitment, with stronger evidence required at each boundary. |
| **Accountable decision** | Who can accept, revise, escalate, or commit the claim? | The architect or review process that owns assumptions, evidence thresholds, risk, and final commitment. |

: **An architectural claim needs more than an artifact:** This claim-focused view of the design-loop card shows why the lighthouse prompt becomes reviewable only when its comparison, scope, evidence, rejection authority, and decision boundary are explicit. {#tbl-architectural-claim-schema tbl-colwidths="[18,33,39]"}

::: {.callout-war-story title="A claim with missing review fields"}
**The claim.** When analyzing AlphaChip's claims using a structured review framework, the generation fields are strong. The intent and task are clear, and the method proposed macro placements at reported expert quality [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].

**The gap.** The critical fields required to establish public trust, such as the baseline, environment, evidence, and rejection authority, were either disputed or unavailable. Because the environment and test cases were not shared, independent researchers could not produce runnable evidence to carry the contested comparison through shared post-route metrics, and no public shared rejection authority could clear the result [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL].

**The lesson.** A claim with those gaps does not say the work is wrong. It says the public claim needs narrower wording or stronger shared evidence, including a shared baseline, a runnable environment, higher-fidelity evidence, and an owner who can reject. State the claim as a review object and the argument stops being about reputation and starts being about the missing fields.
:::

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

This schema also clarifies what AI systems are being asked to do. Generation
can propose artifacts inside $\mathcal{D}$. Prediction can estimate
components of $\mathbf{J}$ before expensive feedback. Optimization can search
tradeoffs under $\mathcal{K}$. Critique and verification can apply $R$.
The architectural result is not any one of those operations. It is the claim
that survives the loop.

::: {#pri-state-the-claim .callout-design-principle title="State the claim as a review object"}
An AI-generated architectural result is credible only when the loop names its workload, baseline,
design space, objective, constraints, evidence, rejection rule, and decision
owner. Until it states those, the AI output is an artifact, not an architectural claim.
:::

## The Design Loop Is the Unit of Analysis

Once the claim is explicit, the architect must define the design loop that can test it.
That shift matters because architecture work is not a single act of generation.
It is a repeated process of framing a problem, choosing abstractions, exploring
alternatives, measuring candidates, rejecting weak results, revising
assumptions, and deciding when evidence is strong enough to commit.

> **Architecture design loop.** An architecture design loop is the repeated
> process that carries architecture state through bounded actions, feedback,
> evidence, rejection, revision, and accountable commitment until it produces an
> artifact or a revised loop.

For Architecture 2.0, this is the loop an AI system enters. If its state,
actions, feedback, and stopping rules are implicit, the system is only
producing outputs, not participating credibly in architecture work.

For the lighthouse prompt, the distinction is immediate. A request for a
low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for XRBench-class mobile XR, a mixed-reality workload suite,
under a 3\ W, 3\ nm-class low-power mobile envelope sounds compact. But the
prompt does not define the design loop. It does not say which workload traces
are authoritative, which vector operations matter, which memory hierarchy is
admissible, which software stack must run, which simulator is trusted, which
power model applies, which process assumptions are available, which
alternatives must be considered, or what evidence is enough to reject a
candidate. These are not details to add after a generative method responds. They are the
architecture problem.

One turn connects represented state to a bounded action, an observation from the
environment, an auditable evidence entry, and a decision to accept, reject,
revise, or escalate. Objectives and constraints bound the turn; stopping and
escalation rules determine whether another turn is justified. The following
equation is only a compact explanation of that update:
$$
s_{t+1} =
\operatorname{Update}(s_t, a_t, o_t, e_t, d_t).
$$
Here, $s_t$ is the represented architecture state, $a_t$ is the bounded
action taken by the loop, $o_t$ is the observation returned by the
environment, $e_t$ is the evidence entry that makes the observation auditable,
and $d_t$ is the human or policy decision to accept, reject,
revise, or escalate. This equation is not a schema and does not claim that every architecture
loop is a Markov decision process[^fn-rl-mdp]. It is a bookkeeping view of the
same turn summarized by the design-loop card. If a loop
cannot say how actions, feedback, evidence, and decisions update state, then
it is not yet represented well enough for credible AI-mediated architecture
work (@fig-design-loop).

[^fn-rl-mdp]: A mathematical framework for modeling decision-making where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of a decision maker; it forms the foundation of reinforcement learning. The chapter borrows the loop's notation from this framework while cautioning that an architecture loop need not literally be one.

![**The Architecture 2.0 design loop:** State, a bounded action, tool feedback, a supporting evidence record, and an accountable decision form one turn, with rejected candidates returned as failed or ruled-out alternatives. A scalar objective or reward may still guide a method inside its bounded role, but it does not decide which claim or commitment advances. That decision follows the declared evidence, rejection authority, and commitment rule. Evidence may move through stages with higher fidelity and cost. Commitment and allowed autonomy remain separate governance choices. Neither rises automatically with fidelity.](images/F3-design-loop){#fig-design-loop width="100%" fig-alt="A left-to-right Architecture 2.0 design loop with boxes for represented state, a bounded action, tool feedback, supporting evidence, and an accountable decision, plus a return path for failed and rejected alternatives. Below the loop, evidence stages may rise in fidelity and cost while rejection authority, the commitment boundary, and allowed autonomy remain separately governed."}

Architecture 2.0 uses that loop as the unit of analysis. A model is one
participant in the loop. It may generate candidates, summarize evidence,
predict outcomes, call tools, critique assumptions, or coordinate subtasks. But
the credibility of the result comes from the whole loop, not from the model in
isolation.

The cross-layer reach of architectural decisions is another reason the loop, not the model, is the unit of
analysis. An architecture decision moves through the whole stack at once. A
choice that is legal at the microarchitecture level can still force a new
compiler pass above it or trip a timing and routing bottleneck below it. The
model that proposes the choice sees only its own layer, while the consequences
ripple up into software and down into physical design. Representing those layers
as intertwined, rather than as isolated abstractions, is part of what the loop
has to carry, because that cross-layer state is what lets a reviewer reject a
candidate that looks valid in one layer but breaks another the model never saw.

## Design Spaces Make Claims Meaningful

An architectural claim is meaningful only relative to a design space. A system
that reports "the best" candidate without exposing the alternatives, invalid
regions, baseline, and tradeoffs has not made an architecture result easy to
review. It has hidden the comparison that gives the result meaning.

In architecture, the design space is not the set of all strings a model might
emit. It is a constrained set of legal choices:
$$
\mathcal{D} = \{x \in X \mid x \text{ is valid under the task, tool chain, and
constraints}\}.
$$
Here, $X$ is that unconstrained space of candidate artifacts, everything a
method could emit. The validity conditions may include ISA compatibility, memory semantics,
software support, timing assumptions, power limits, package constraints,
verification requirements, and deployment policy. A candidate outside
$\mathcal{D}$ is not a bold design. It is an invalid action unless the loop
explicitly revises the design space and records why.

The lighthouse prompt makes this concrete. A 64-bit RISC-V-based mobile XR
subsystem might vary vector width, cache and memory hierarchy, accelerator
partitioning, dataflow, clocking, voltage assumptions, compiler/runtime
support, and verification scope. Some choices are legal but unattractive. Some
are attractive under a proxy but fail at higher fidelity. Some violate the
power envelope, process assumptions, software contract, or workload coverage.
An Architecture 2.0 loop must represent those distinctions. Otherwise it cannot
know whether it is improving a design or exploiting a hole in the problem
statement.

The design space is also where multiobjective efficiency enters the ontology. The objective
is rarely a scalar reward. It is a vector of performance, energy, latency,
area, reliability, programmability, verification burden, cost, and evidence
requirements. A design-space report is therefore an evidence object. It should
show what was explored, what was rejected, what tradeoffs remain, and what the
architect must still decide.

## The Architecture 2.0 Ontology {#sec-arch2-ontology}

A method label is not enough to review an architecture claim. A paper can say
it uses an LLM, reinforcement learning, Bayesian optimization[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimization], or a surrogate
model and still leave the important relationships invisible. The reader does
not yet know what task was bounded, what actions were legal, what feedback was
used, what failed, or who accepted the commitment. A taxonomy can group methods,
benchmarks, tools, or tasks, but those categories will change with the field.

[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimization]: A strategy for optimizing expensive black-box functions, where each candidate evaluation (such as a cycle-accurate simulation) is costly. The chapter names it as one method a paper might cite, and stresses that the label matters less than the loop state, available evaluation resources, and rejection rules the method operates under.

> **Architecture 2.0 ontology.** The Architecture 2.0 ontology names the
> entities and relationships that must exist for AI-mediated architecture work to
> be represented, acted on, evaluated, rejected, and advanced with decision
> rights visible.

As a preview of @sec-data-representations-world-models, a world model is the loop's
architecture-scoped belief about how actions change outcomes
[@HaSchmidhuber2018WorldModels; @SuttonBarto2018ReinforcementLearning]. It may
live in a simulator, surrogate, cost model, or design rules.
@sec-data-representations-world-models gives the canonical definition and
treatment.

The important content is relational. Intent constrains tasks.
Tasks determine what must be represented. Representation limits what the loop
can observe and modify. The world model, previewed here before @sec-data-representations-world-models, encodes beliefs about how actions
change outcomes. Tools and environments define valid actions and measurable
feedback. Methods are selected for the task, representation, and feedback
limits. Feedback becomes evidence only when fidelity, provenance, uncertainty,
and relevance are understood. Declared decision processes accept, reject,
revise, or escalate the result. The ontology names those relationships. The design-loop
card records them; there is no separate ontology form to complete.

The supporting evidence record captures what the loop actually observed and how that
observation affected the claim. @sec-feedback-verification-trust owns the full
treatment of feedback and evidence.

This is why ontology should precede taxonomy. Before classifying whether a paper
uses an LLM[^fn-ml-llm], reinforcement learning[^fn-rl-method-label], Bayesian
optimization, a surrogate, or a simulator wrapper, identify the exposed loop.
Once its task, actions, feedback, evidence, rejection, and decision relationships
are visible, method categories become useful. Before that, labels can hide more
than they reveal.

[^fn-ml-llm]: Large Language Model, a neural network trained on vast amounts of text to understand and generate human-like language. Here it names one method label a paper might advertise, which the chapter argues reveals little until the exposed loop state is identified.

[^fn-rl-method-label]: For Architecture 2.0, the label "reinforcement learning" still does not say what state the agent saw, which actions were legal, which reward proxied the objective, or which rejection conditions bounded the loop.

The practical implication is not to build a chip-specific language model[^fn-ml-language-model] first.
A field becomes AI-addressable when its objects of work, legal actions,
feedback, evidence, rejection rules, and commitment boundaries are represented
well enough for methods to act and experts to judge. That is why this book
starts with the design loop rather than a catalog of models.

[^fn-ml-language-model]: A statistical model trained to predict the next word or token in a sequence, forming the foundation of modern AI text generation. The chapter's point is that building such a domain-specific model is not the precondition for AI-addressable architecture; representing the loop's objects, actions, and evidence is.

## The Compact Framework

@fig-ontology-chain shows the chapter's argument in one view. Intent bounds the
task. Representation, tools, and method assignments bound what the loop can know and
do. Evidence, rejection, and decision authority bound what it may claim.

![**The Architecture 2.0 ontology chain makes the loop explicit:** Intent, task, and design space define what work the loop is allowed to do; representation and world model define what the loop can know; tools and environments define valid action and feedback; compound methods act inside the loop; evidence and accountable decision determine whether an artifact is accepted, rejected, or used to revise the loop.](images/F4-ontology-chain){#fig-ontology-chain width="100%" fig-alt="Process diagram connecting intent, task, design space, representation, environment, methods, evidence, and accountable decision into an explicit Architecture 2.0 loop."}

The change is that the loop, not the model, becomes the review object. For
practical work, use the twelve-field design-loop card. The claim tuple, state
equation, figures, and grouped questions in this chapter explain different
relationships inside that card; none is another schema.

The card can be read in three passes. The first establishes scope through
intent, task, and design space. The second exposes execution through
representation, environment, method assignment, and evaluation limits. The
third judges the result through evidence, failed or rejected alternatives,
rejection authority, commitment boundary, and decision.
@tbl-compact-framework-checklist applies those passes to the lighthouse prompt.

| **Reading pass** | **Reader question** | **Lighthouse instance** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Scope** | What claim is being made, for which workload and baseline, inside which legal design space? | Improve mobile XR efficiency within a 3\ W, 3\ nm-class low-power mobile envelope while keeping the workload, baseline, legal RISC-V choices, and deferred work explicit. |
| **Execution** | What state can the loop read and change, what job does each method perform, and what feedback can it afford? | Workload traces, architecture parameters, compiler assumptions, a simulator or cost model, bounded candidate generation or search, and a declared evaluation budget. |
| **Judgment** | What supports or rejects the claim, what failed, and what commitment follows? | Pareto and sensitivity evidence, rejected candidates, a stated rejection authority, and a bounded architectural decision. |

: **The card can be read in three passes:** Scope, execution, and judgment group the canonical card fields for review without creating another schema. {#tbl-compact-framework-checklist tbl-colwidths="[20,36,34]"}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

These reading passes are intentionally stricter than many current
demonstrations. A system can be useful while still leaving its action bounds,
evidence standard, rejection authority, or commitment underspecified.

The practical artifact is the design-loop card developed below and expanded in
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card. The card is not a new concept
on top of the framework. @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card also gives the card a
machine-checkable schema and four conformance levels, from context-only to an
independently rejectable loop, so "we used the card" becomes a claim a tool and a
reviewer can check rather than a gesture.

The translation aid in @tbl-architecture-translation-matrix keeps the vocabulary from drifting into generic AI
language. Words such as state, action, observation, environment, reward, and
critic are useful only after they are translated into architecture objects
and checks. It is not another review form.
If a paper says an automated method acts in an environment, the reader should be able to
name the architecture state it reads, the action it is allowed to take, the
tool feedback it observes, and the authority that can reject the result.

| **Generic term** | **Architecture translation** | **Example artifacts or observations** | **What can reject it** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **State** | Workload, design, software, tool, constraint, and evidence state. | Traces, configs, RTL, compiler IR, simulator stats, EDA reports, review notes. | Missing provenance or hidden assumptions. |
| **Action** | Legal architecture, compiler, runtime, or tool-flow change. | Change cache size, vector width, mapping, schedule, constraint, partition, or test. | Invalid parameter, noncompilable code, nonsynthesizable RTL, or policy violation. |
| **Observation** | Feedback returned by a tool, benchmark, review, or deployment path. | Latency, energy, area, timing, congestion, warnings, failures, telemetry. | Wrong workload, stale tool version, simulator mismatch, or weak fidelity. |
| **Environment** | Tool-connected harness that defines legal actions and feedback. | Simulator wrapper, compiler pipeline, RTL flow, EDA stage, benchmark harness. | Unmodeled constraints, nondeterminism, incomplete logging, or invalid actions. |
| **Objective** | Explicit architecture tradeoff, not a generic reward. | Performance, power, and area; tail latency; power envelope; reliability; carbon; cost; evidence budget. | Proxy gaming, lost Pareto tradeoff, or missing accountable decision rule. |
| **Critic/verifier** | Independent check that can challenge or reject a claim. | Tests, formal checks, baseline replay, cross-simulator comparison, signoff review. | Unsupported claim, failed check, counterexample, or insufficient evidence. |

: **AI loop terms need architecture translations:** Architecture 2.0 uses generic loop vocabulary only when each term is grounded in concrete hardware/software design objects, tool outputs, and rejection mechanisms. {#tbl-architecture-translation-matrix tbl-colwidths="[16,28,28,18]"}

These relationships do not form a pipeline that runs once. A failed
simulation may revise the representation. A weak benchmark result may revise
the task. A provenance problem may invalidate the evidence. A rejection may
change the environment, not merely reject a candidate. Architecture 2.0 is
therefore not only about adding AI into existing work. It is about designing the
loop so that AI participation is bounded, observable, and accountable.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Loop Revision Gate"}
When an AI method fails or produces an invalid result, the architect must make a decision at the loop boundary. Does the failure simply reject the candidate, or does it require revising the environment, design space, or evidence standard to keep the method bounded?
:::

## Autonomy Is Earned, Not Declared

Autonomous or non-autonomous is too coarse a description. Autonomy is a property
of a bounded loop, not a personality trait of a model. @fig-autonomy-ladder-human-agent
shows four representative stages, from assisted exploration to bounded
autonomous action. The stages are not another schema or a maturity score. Read
them through the same card. Broader authority is credible only when the loop
also exposes allowed actions, feedback and evidence, rejection or escalation,
and the commitment boundary. The same test applies whether one automated
participant acts or several coordinate.

![**Autonomy is earned by the loop:** Higher autonomy is a property of a bounded loop with explicit actions, feedback, evidence, rollback or escalation paths, rejection authority, and architect-owned commitment boundaries.](images/F4c-autonomy-ladder-human-agent){#fig-autonomy-ladder-human-agent width="100%" fig-alt="Ladder diagram showing autonomy rising only as actions, feedback, evidence, rollback or escalation paths, rejection authority, and architect-owned commitments become explicit."}

The appropriate stage depends on architecture-specific risk. A compiler flag
that can be rolled back after telemetry is not the same as an RTL change that
affects timing closure. A simulator configuration is not the same as a
mask-level choice. A benchmark-generation loop is not the same as a signoff
loop. The more irreversible the action, the stronger the evidence and
rejection authority must be.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint}
Before granting a loop broader authority, use its card to locate the allowed
actions, evidence requirement, rejection or escalation path, and commitment it
may not cross. If any is missing, the autonomy is declared, not earned.
:::

The remaining sections preview conditions developed in
@sec-data-representations-world-models,
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces,
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization, and
@sec-feedback-verification-trust.

## Intent Defines the Task

Architecture tasks do not appear naturally. They are carved out of messy
intent. A product goal such as "improve mobile XR efficiency" is not yet a task.
It must become bounded work, such as characterizing a workload, comparing
compute organizations, estimating memory traffic, or preparing a design-space
report.

This translation is architectural judgment. It decides what is in scope, what
is out of scope, what can be measured, and what cost of being wrong is
acceptable. It also sets how ambitious the loop may be. Critiquing a report,
predicting energy, searching an accelerator tiling space, and editing RTL need
different state, feedback, and rejection conditions. "Use AI" is therefore
never the task. The card must bound the work before a method is chosen;
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization later develops the bounded jobs
that methods can perform.

## Representations and World Models

Representation is the first hard problem because it determines what the loop
can see. Architecture knowledge lives in many forms, including natural-language
specifications, ISA documents, traces, graphs, simulator configurations, RTL,
compiler IR, EDA reports, benchmark metadata, scripts, and review notes. Hidden
defaults, undocumented constraints, and forgotten rejections create state an
automated loop cannot inspect. The result may be invalid, misleading, or
impossible to replay even when the generated artifact looks plausible.

This preview follows the distinction defined in @sec-data-representations-world-models: a world model is different from a representation. The representation says what
is encoded; the previewed world model says what the loop believes will happen after an
action. A simulator, learned surrogate, calibrated equation, or design rule may
play that role, and each has a limited scope and failure mode.

QuArch, an architecture-centric benchmark dataset, makes the boundary concrete.
It can assess whether a model knows architecture concepts, but not whether an
automated participant has the represented workload, tool, action, evidence, and rejection state
needed to synthesize or reject a candidate
[@PrakashEtAl2025QuArch]. That distinction is the bridge to
@sec-data-representations-world-models.

## Tools Become Environments

Architecture tools become Architecture 2.0 environments when they define how an
automated participant or method can act. A simulator, compiler, profiler, RTL tool, EDA flow,
runtime system, or fleet telemetry pipeline does more than return a number. It
defines which actions are legal, how long feedback takes, what observations are
available, what costs are incurred, what provenance is recorded, and what
failure means.

The distinction becomes a visual test (@fig-tools-become-environments-preview). A wrapper connects a method to a tool, while an environment exposes the
contract that lets the loop audit action, feedback, failure, and rejection.

![**Tools become environments when the wrapper exposes a contract:** A tool wrapper is useful only when it returns more than a score. It must define legal actions, observations, cost and fidelity, provenance, invalid-action semantics, and rejection conditions so feedback can become architecture evidence.](images/F4e-tools-become-environments-preview){#fig-tools-become-environments-preview width="100%" fig-alt="Side-by-side diagram contrasting a basic tool wrapper that calls a tool and returns a score with an architecture environment that exposes legal actions, observations, cost and fidelity, provenance, invalid-action semantics, and rejection conditions."}

ArchGym, an open-source evaluation environment, is an important example because
it treats the connection between search algorithms and architecture simulators as
a first-class interface [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. Its durable lesson is that a
simulator wrapper becomes architectural only when it exposes legal actions,
invalid-action semantics, feedback cost, provenance, comparable baselines, and
rejection conditions. @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces expands
this point and asks what such environments can and cannot prove.

## Agents and Methods Have Roles in a Compound System

The word "agent" can hide too much. In credible Architecture 2.0 systems, there
may be several roles rather than one monolithic actor. A generator proposes
candidates. A predictor estimates behavior before expensive evaluation. An
optimizer chooses what to try next. A critic challenges assumptions. A verifier
checks constraints. A planner decomposes work. A tool caller executes actions.
A coordinator tracks state, provenance, and dependencies. An architect or
accountable engineering team sets intent, declares decision rights, and decides
what evidence is enough for consequential claims.

Language models, search procedures, learned surrogates, scripts, formal tools,
and people can fill those roles in different combinations. The review question
is not which mechanism is fashionable. It is what role acts, what state it
consumes, what action it takes, and what can reject its output.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization develops these compositions.

## Feedback Becomes Evidence

Feedback is not evidence by default. A simulator result, benchmark score,
synthesis report, generated explanation, or model confidence value is feedback.
It becomes evidence only when it is tied to a claim, a decision, and a
provenance trail.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="A 3 W claim needs evidence, not a number"}
**Context.** A reported power result is feedback. It becomes evidence only when
the loop records enough state for a reviewer to judge what the number means.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** If a generative method claims that a "vector-capable CPU,
accelerator, or SoC block" for the "XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload"
meets the "3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP mobile process," the loop must
record the workload, input distribution, memory traffic, power model, process
assumptions, compiled software stack, rejected alternatives, uncertainty, and
rejection rule.

**Evidence rule.** The same power number has different authority as a proxy
estimate, cycle-level simulation, synthesis result, post-layout estimate, or
silicon measurement. A proxy estimate may support exploration; post-layout or
silicon evidence is needed before stronger implementation or deployment
commitments.

**Takeaway.** The design-space report must say which evidence level supports the
claim and which commitment boundary it has not crossed.
:::

Evidence also includes negative information. Rejected candidates, failed
simulator runs, invalid configurations, proxy wins that disappear at higher
fidelity, and assumptions that had to be abandoned are not waste. They are
architecture data. They help explain why a surviving candidate deserves
attention and what would overturn the claim. @sec-feedback-verification-trust
develops the evidence, fidelity, and rejection rules this preview only names.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Feedback vs. Evidence Gate"}
Before committing a model-generated candidate, locate the card's evidence
pointer, the result or check that could invalidate the candidate, and the owner
of rejection authority. If these are undefined, the output cannot pass the
commitment boundary.
:::

## The Design-Loop Card

> **Design-loop card.** A design-loop card is a compact, inspectable summary of
> an architectural claim and the loop behind it. It states the scope, permitted
> actions, method assignments, available feedback, supporting and contrary
> evidence, rejection path, and accountable next decision while leaving detailed
> evidence and replay artifacts in linked records.

The ontology becomes operational through the design-loop card. The card is the
practical payload of the ontology and the only reader-facing schema introduced
by this chapter. It gives a compact way to describe a paper, project, tool,
benchmark, or internal loop. Readers fill this card, not the claim tuple, state
equation, ontology chain, or grouped reading passes.

A compact example for the lighthouse prompt appears in @fig-design-loop-card-example. The point is not that the card completes the design. The
point is that it exposes the state a credible loop must carry before any
generated candidate should be trusted.

![**A filled design-loop card turns a prompt into reviewable state:** The lighthouse prompt becomes explicit loop state: intent, task, design space, representation, environment, method role, feedback budget, evidence, failed runs and rejected alternatives, rejection authority, commitment boundary, and accountable decision.](images/F4b-design-loop-card-example){#fig-design-loop-card-example width="100%" fig-alt="Filled design-loop card mapping the lighthouse prompt into all twelve canonical fields: intent, task, design space, representation, environment, method role, feedback budget, evidence, failed runs and rejected alternatives, rejection authority, commitment boundary, and accountable decision."}

The card is deliberately simple. Its purpose is not to create paperwork. Its
purpose is to reveal whether a claimed Architecture 2.0 contribution exposes
the loop that makes it credible. A paper that reports a better search result
but hides the cost and availability of feedback, rejected candidates, or
environment validity is hard to compare. A tool that produces designs but cannot say what rejects them
is hard to trust. A benchmark that measures model accuracy but not
architecture-relevant reasoning may be useful, but it should not be mistaken
for a complete design-loop evaluation.
Missing card fields are review outcomes, not formatting problems. Ask for
evidence, escalate fidelity, reject the claim, or narrow the commitment until
the loop can support what it reports.

@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives the full 12-field card and review
rubric. Detailed evidence and runnable receipts remain linked artifacts rather
than additional card fields. The important point here is that every major
Architecture 2.0 claim should be able to expose its loop through this one
review object.

## How the Rest of the Book Uses the Ontology

The remaining chapters unpack the ontology as missing conditions for credible
AI participation. @sec-data-representations-world-models asks what
architecture data must encode before AI systems can reason about it.
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces asks how simulators, compilers,
EDA flows, benchmarks, and deployment systems become action settings.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization asks which bounded method
assignments are valid under available evaluation resources.
@sec-feedback-verification-trust asks when
feedback becomes evidence strong enough for rejection and commitment.
@sec-running-the-loop runs one loop end to end on the lighthouse prompt.
@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack applies the framework across loop patterns in software, architecture
DSE, co-design, systems, and high-commitment silicon-facing work. @sec-what-architect-owns
returns to the architect, outlining what remains nondelegable, what the community must
build, and what it would mean for Architecture 2.0 to become a discipline
rather than a collection of demonstrations.

## Open Research Questions

The ontology turns the pressure from the previous chapter into a review object.
The research questions here ask which fields make an AI-assisted architecture
claim inspectable, which workload and baseline records make the claim credible,
and which parts of the design-loop card can be checked before a loop is
executable.

1. **What minimum card content makes AI-assisted architecture work reviewable?**
An AI-assisted architecture claim should expose its task, workload scope,
baseline, represented state, environment, method assignments, supporting
evidence, rejection authority, and commitment boundary. A strong contribution would test
how much of that content a design-loop card can index without requiring the
author's full internal toolchain. The evaluation should measure whether
independent reviewers identify missing evidence, unsupported commitments, and
unreviewable loop transitions more consistently than they do from a normal
paper artifact. The design-loop card in @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives
the starting point.

2. **What workload and baseline records make a claim credible?**
Workload characterization becomes reviewable when a paper records input
distributions, benchmark construction, excluded cases, holdout workloads,
software versions, baseline assumptions, and provenance. A paper-quality result
would define a workload and baseline packet for AI-assisted architecture claims,
then test whether reviewers can tell where a result generalizes and where it
does not. This question sharpens the ontology by making workload scope part of
the claim rather than background context.

3. **How can holdout benchmarks expose loop overfitting?**
An AI-assisted loop can overfit to a benchmark suite, a trace slice, a proxy
model, or a reviewer-visible metric. A strong research artifact would define
holdout construction methods for architecture loops and evaluate whether they
catch design-space lessons that fail on shifted workloads, changed baselines,
or stronger feedback. The result should tell reviewers when a loop has learned
an architectural relationship and when it has learned the evaluation surface.

4. **How can rejected alternatives become auditable design-space evidence?**
Rejected candidates are part of the claim because they show which regions of
the design space were considered and why they failed. A research contribution
would define a record for rejected alternatives, invalid actions, failed
timing closures, proxy mismatches, and repaired candidates, then test whether
reviewers can reconstruct a design-space boundary from that record. This is the
negative-evidence version of the ontology in @sec-arch2-ontology.

5. **What can a design-loop card catch before execution?**
A design-loop card should be useful before a loop runs, not only after a result
exists. A strong paper would evaluate which card fields predict later failure
modes such as missing baselines, invalid action spaces, unsupported evidence,
or commitment boundaries that are too broad. The paper object could be a static
checker, review protocol, or authoring tool that flags under-specified claims
early while leaving architectural judgment with human reviewers.

## Conclusion

This chapter asked what a design loop must make explicit before an AI output can
be accepted or rejected as an architectural claim. The continuity with classical
practice is the loop itself, since architecture has always advanced through
cycles of abstraction, measurement, feedback, and judgment. The discontinuity is
automation, and with it a change in the failure mode, from slow human drift to
fast, confident, catastrophic error when verification and rejection go missing.

The ontology answers that question by naming the minimum a claim must expose to
be reviewable. A claim has to show the task and state it represents, the
environment it acts in, the method assignments that produced it, the evidence
drawn from feedback, the authority that can reject it, and the strongest next
decision that evidence supports. Stated as a review object rather than a result,
a claim can be compared, inspected, and contested by someone who never ran the
loop. Replay or reproduction requires a separate runnable receipt that binds
executable inputs, environment and tool versions, parameters, and evidence
artifacts to the card. The design-loop card is simply the review object made
routine.

The ontology guarantees no correctness, and that is the point. It does not pick
winning models or tools. It exposes what must be represented, measured, checked,
rejected, and decided, which is exactly why it can outlast whichever models and
tools are current. A claim that cannot expose the loop that produced it is not
yet an architectural claim, however good its numbers look.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** An architectural claim is reviewed through the loop that produced it, and the design-loop card is where that loop becomes inspectable.
- **Reader test:** Can you review an AI-generated architectural claim through
  the loop that produced it, including its environment, method assignments,
  supporting evidence, rejection authority, and commitment boundary?
- **Up next:** The preview continues in @sec-data-representations-world-models, which asks what representations and world models must encode before an AI-assisted loop can act on architecture state credibly.
:::
